items.prompt_md: In the monotonic-stack solution to Daily Temperatures, what do we keep on the stack?
options: The temperatures themselves, sorted from coldest to warmest
Indices of days that are still waiting for a warmer day
Indices of days that have already found their warmer day
A running maximum temperature seen so far
items.answer: Indices of days that are still waiting for a warmer day
items.explanation_md: The stack holds the indices whose answer isn't known yet. The moment a warmer day arrives, we pop and resolve every index it beats — that's why we store indices (to compute `i - j`), not temperatures.

items.prompt_md: For every element in an array you need the distance to the next element that is strictly greater than it, and a nested-loop rescan from each index would be O(n^2). Which approach fits?
options: Sort the values, then binary-search for each element's neighbor
Slide a window and shrink it from the left whenever a condition breaks
Keep a stack of indices still waiting for a bigger value, popping and resolving them as bigger values arrive
Build a hash map from value to its first occurrence
items.answer: Keep a stack of indices still waiting for a bigger value, popping and resolving them as bigger values arrive
items.explanation_md: "Next greater element, single left-to-right pass" is the monotonic-stack fingerprint: each index is pushed once and popped at most once, so the whole array costs O(n) even though any one day can resolve several waiting days at once.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the monotonic-stack solution to Daily Temperatures?
items.explanation_md: Every index is pushed exactly once and popped at most once across the entire run, so the total work is O(n) time and O(n) worst-case space, even though a single day can trigger a burst of pops.
